title: Team queue progress is preserved as additive overlays instead of rewriting the canonical queue
summary: The shared state declares that teams update only owned records, while later continuity work records completed slices under operations/queue-updates and explicitly marks canonical_mutation_performed false. The durable operating pattern is therefore an event-like additive overlay: ACTIVE_QUEUE.json remains a baseline snapshot, and subsequent owned queue-update records preserve later progress without erasing prior states or risking cross-team overwrite. Consumers must reconcile the baseline with later additive records before describing current queue status.

status_relations.observed: CURRENT_STATE defines shared-state coordination; ACTIVE_QUEUE remains an older canonical snapshot; M-006 records later completion as an additive owned file and states that the canonical queue was not mutated.
status_relations.inferred: The repository is functioning as an append-oriented state log even though no formal reducer or materialized-current-state builder is yet identified.
status_relations.proposed: Add a deterministic queue reducer that loads ACTIVE_QUEUE.json plus operations/queue-updates in commit or declared-event order and emits a non-authoritative materialized view with provenance for every field.
status_relations.superseded: Reading ACTIVE_QUEUE.json alone as the complete current queue state is no longer a reliable interpretation once additive updates exist.
status_relations.unresolved: No committed conflict-resolution rule was observed for two additive updates that target the same queue item with incompatible status or progress fields.

falsification_or_review_route: Fetch the three indexed file blobs and the queue-update commit. Falsify the observed claim by showing that later team progress is routinely written back into ACTIVE_QUEUE.json rather than retained additively, or that the M-006 queue-update file does not explicitly preserve the canonical queue. Review the inferred architectural claim separately by locating a committed reducer or authoritative ordering rule that already materializes additive updates.
